question: Under 40 CFR §82.157(a), what is the maximum allowable annual leak rate for a comfort cooling (air conditioning) system before a repair obligation is triggered?
options: 10% of the full charge per year
20% of the full charge per year
15% of the full charge per year
5% of the full charge per year
answer_text: 10% of the full charge per year
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(a), comfort cooling appliances must be repaired when the annual leak rate exceeds 10% of the full refrigerant charge. This is a stricter threshold than commercial refrigeration (20%) because comfort cooling systems are generally easier to maintain and seal. The annual leak rate is calculated as: (lbs leaked per year ÷ full charge in lbs) × 100.

question: A comfort cooling system has a full charge of 100 lbs. During the past 12 months, 12 lbs of refrigerant was added to maintain the charge. What is the annual leak rate, and does it trigger a repair obligation?
options: 12%; yes, it exceeds the 10% threshold and must be repaired
12%; no, the system is below the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold
10%; no, it is exactly at the limit and no repair is needed
8%; no, it is below the 10% threshold
answer_text: 12%; yes, it exceeds the 10% threshold and must be repaired
explanation: Annual leak rate = (lbs leaked ÷ full charge) × 100 = (12 ÷ 100) × 100 = 12%. Since 12% exceeds the 10% threshold for comfort cooling systems under 40 CFR §82.157(a), a repair obligation is triggered. The technician or owner must repair the leak within 30 days of discovery.

question: Under the AIM Act HFC Leak Repair Rule effective in 2026, what is the new minimum charge size threshold that subjects a comfort cooling system to the leak repair requirements?
options: 15 lbs
50 lbs
25 lbs
100 lbs
answer_text: 15 lbs
explanation: Prior to 2026, only comfort cooling systems containing 50 lbs or more of refrigerant were subject to the leak repair requirements under 40 CFR §82.157(a). The AIM Act HFC Leak Repair Rule, effective 2026, lowers this threshold to 15 lbs, meaning significantly more systems are now subject to the 10% annual leak rate limit and repair obligations.
source_ref: 40 CFR §82.157(a); AIM Act HFC Leak Repair Rule (2026)

question: Before the 2026 AIM Act HFC Leak Repair Rule update, what was the minimum refrigerant charge size required for a comfort cooling system to be subject to the EPA leak repair obligations under 40 CFR §82.157?
options: 50 lbs
15 lbs
25 lbs
100 lbs
answer_text: 50 lbs
explanation: Prior to the 2026 regulatory update under the AIM Act, the EPA's leak repair requirements at 40 CFR §82.157 applied only to comfort cooling appliances containing 50 lbs or more of refrigerant. The 2026 update reduces this threshold to 15 lbs, expanding coverage to many smaller systems that were previously exempt.

question: A rooftop comfort cooling unit contains 40 lbs of R-410A and leaked 5 lbs over the past year. As of 2026, is the owner required to repair the leak?
options: Yes, because the 2026 threshold is 15 lbs and the annual leak rate is 12.5%, exceeding 10%
No, because the system contains less than 50 lbs and is exempt
No, because 5 lbs leaked is below the 10 lb repair threshold
Yes, because any leak in a comfort cooling system must be repaired immediately
answer_text: Yes, because the 2026 threshold is 15 lbs and the annual leak rate is 12.5%, exceeding 10%
explanation: As of 2026, the AIM Act HFC Leak Repair Rule lowered the charge threshold from 50 lbs to 15 lbs. This 40 lb system now falls under the regulation. The annual leak rate = (5 ÷ 40) × 100 = 12.5%, which exceeds the 10% comfort cooling threshold under 40 CFR §82.157(a). A repair obligation is therefore triggered.
source_ref: 40 CFR §82.157(a); AIM Act HFC Leak Repair Rule (2026)

question: How is the annual leak rate calculated for a comfort cooling system under EPA regulations?
options: (lbs leaked per year ÷ full charge in lbs) × 100
(full charge ÷ lbs leaked) × 100
(lbs added per service call ÷ full charge) × 100
(lbs leaked × 12 months) ÷ full charge
answer_text: (lbs leaked per year ÷ full charge in lbs) × 100
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(a), the annual leak rate is expressed as a percentage calculated by dividing the total pounds of refrigerant leaked during a 12-month period by the full system charge in pounds, then multiplying by 100. For example, a 100 lb system that leaked 10 lbs has a 10% annual leak rate — exactly at the comfort cooling threshold.

question: A comfort cooling system has a full charge of 80 lbs. The service log shows that 7 lbs of refrigerant was added over the last year. Does the annual leak rate trigger a repair obligation?
options: No, the annual leak rate is 8.75%, which is below the 10% threshold
Yes, the annual leak rate is 8.75%, which exceeds the 10% threshold
Yes, because any system that requires recharge must be repaired
No, because only systems containing 50 lbs or more are subject to the rule
answer_text: No, the annual leak rate is 8.75%, which is below the 10% threshold
explanation: Annual leak rate = (7 ÷ 80) × 100 = 8.75%. This is below the 10% threshold for comfort cooling systems under 40 CFR §82.157(a), so no repair obligation is triggered at this time. However, technicians should still note the leak and monitor it. Note: as of 2026, this 80 lb system is subject to the rule since it exceeds the new 15 lb charge threshold.

question: Which statement accurately compares the pre-2026 and post-2026 charge size thresholds for EPA leak repair obligations applied to comfort cooling systems?
options: Pre-2026: 50 lbs minimum; Post-2026: 15 lbs minimum under the AIM Act
Pre-2026: 15 lbs minimum; Post-2026: 50 lbs minimum
Pre-2026: 25 lbs minimum; Post-2026: 10 lbs minimum
The threshold has always been 50 lbs and has not changed
answer_text: Pre-2026: 50 lbs minimum; Post-2026: 15 lbs minimum under the AIM Act
explanation: The AIM Act HFC Leak Repair Rule, effective in 2026, reduced the charge size threshold from 50 lbs to 15 lbs for comfort cooling appliances subject to 40 CFR §82.157 leak repair requirements. This change significantly expands the scope of covered equipment, requiring owners of smaller A/C systems to comply with the 10% annual leak rate limit and associated repair deadlines.
source_ref: 40 CFR §82.157(a); AIM Act HFC Leak Repair Rule (2026)

question: Under 40 CFR §82.157(b), how many days does the owner of a comfort cooling or commercial refrigeration system have to repair a refrigerant leak after it is discovered?
options: 30 days
60 days
120 days
15 days
answer_text: 30 days
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(b), the standard repair deadline is 30 days after the leak is discovered for comfort cooling and commercial refrigeration appliances. The 30-day clock starts when the leak is identified, not when it first began. Failure to repair within 30 days without an approved exception constitutes a violation of EPA regulations.

question: An industrial process refrigeration system is discovered to have a leak that exceeds the regulatory threshold. The owner determines that a full repair within 30 days is technically infeasible. What is the maximum extension available under EPA regulations?
options: 120-day extension, provided the owner applies for it
60-day extension, automatically granted
90-day extension, granted by the EPA regional office
No extension is available; repair must be completed in 30 days
answer_text: 120-day extension, provided the owner applies for it
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.157(b)(2), industrial process refrigeration systems are eligible for a 120-day extension beyond the standard 30-day deadline if the owner can demonstrate that repair within 30 days is technically infeasible. This exception applies only to industrial process refrigeration — not comfort cooling or standard commercial refrigeration. The owner must formally apply for this extension.

question: If a comfort cooling system leak cannot be repaired within 30 days, what action must the owner take as required by 40 CFR §82.157?
options: Develop a retrofit or retirement plan within 30 days
Apply for a 120-day industrial process refrigeration extension
Immediately retire the equipment and recover all refrigerant
Continue operating and repair at the next scheduled maintenance
answer_text: Develop a retrofit or retirement plan within 30 days
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(b), if repair of a comfort cooling or commercial refrigeration appliance cannot be completed within 30 days of leak discovery, the owner must develop a retrofit or retirement plan within that same 30-day window. This plan must then be executed, and the full retrofit or replacement must be completed within 1 year per §82.157(c). The 120-day extension is only available for industrial process refrigeration systems.

question: Under 40 CFR §82.157(c), what is the maximum timeframe allowed to complete a retrofit or replacement plan for an appliance with an unrepaired refrigerant leak?
options: 1 year from when the plan was developed
6 months from when the leak was discovered
90 days from leak discovery
2 years with EPA approval
answer_text: 1 year from when the plan was developed
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(c), when an owner cannot repair a leak within 30 days and must instead develop a retrofit or retirement plan, that plan must be fully executed within 1 year. This provides a structured path to compliance for complex systems where immediate repair is not feasible, while ensuring equipment is ultimately brought into compliance or retired.

question: A supermarket refrigeration system (commercial refrigeration) is found leaking above the 20% threshold. What is the standard repair deadline under EPA regulations?
options: 30 days after the leak is discovered
120 days after the leak is discovered
60 days after the leak is discovered
Immediately upon discovery
answer_text: 30 days after the leak is discovered
explanation: The standard 30-day repair deadline under 40 CFR §82.157(b) applies to both comfort cooling and commercial refrigeration appliances. Supermarket refrigeration systems are commercial refrigeration, not industrial process refrigeration, so they do not qualify for the 120-day extension. The repair must be completed within 30 days of leak discovery.

question: Which type of refrigeration system is eligible for the 120-day repair extension under 40 CFR §82.157(b)(2)?
options: Industrial process refrigeration only
Both commercial refrigeration and industrial process refrigeration
Comfort cooling systems only
Any system containing more than 50 lbs of refrigerant
answer_text: Industrial process refrigeration only
explanation: The 120-day extension under 40 CFR §82.157(b)(2) is exclusively available for industrial process refrigeration systems where repair within 30 days is technically infeasible. Comfort cooling appliances and commercial refrigeration systems (such as supermarket cases and walk-in coolers) must comply with the standard 30-day repair deadline, or the owner must develop a retrofit/retirement plan within that 30-day period.

question: An industrial process refrigeration system leak is discovered on January 1. The owner applies for and is granted a 120-day extension. By what date must the repair be completed?
options: May 1 (approximately 120 days after January 1)
March 1 (30 days after January 1)
July 1 (180 days after January 1)
January 1 of the following year
answer_text: May 1 (approximately 120 days after January 1)
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.157(b)(2), the 120-day extension begins from the date the leak is discovered. If the leak was discovered on January 1, the 120-day period extends to approximately May 1 (January = 31 days remaining after Jan 1, February = 28 days, March = 31 days, April = 30 days = 120 days total). This extension is only available for industrial process refrigeration when repair within 30 days is technically infeasible.

question: A comfort cooling system leak is discovered on March 1. The owner cannot complete the repair by the standard deadline. What two things must the owner do under 40 CFR §82.157?
options: Develop a retrofit/retirement plan by March 31 and complete it within 1 year
Apply for a 120-day extension and submit a retrofit plan to the EPA
Immediately recover all refrigerant and shut down the system
Notify the EPA within 5 days and repair within 60 days
answer_text: Develop a retrofit/retirement plan by March 31 and complete it within 1 year
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(b) and §82.157(c), when a comfort cooling system cannot be repaired within 30 days of leak discovery (March 1 + 30 days = March 31), the owner must develop a retrofit or retirement plan within that same 30-day window. Once developed, the plan must be completed within 1 year. The 120-day extension is not available for comfort cooling — only for industrial process refrigeration.

question: What does a pressure-temperature (P-T) chart show for a given refrigerant?
options: The saturation temperature at a given pressure
The superheat required at the evaporator outlet
The maximum operating pressure of the system
The subcooling temperature at the condenser outlet
answer_text: The saturation temperature at a given pressure
explanation: A P-T (Pressure-Temperature) chart shows the relationship between saturation pressure and saturation temperature for a specific refrigerant. At any given pressure, the chart tells you the temperature at which the refrigerant will change phase (boil or condense). Technicians use P-T charts to verify system operation, check for non-condensables, and identify refrigerant type by comparing measured pressure and temperature values.
source_ref: ASHRAE Refrigeration Fundamentals; 40 CFR §82 Technician Guide

question: A manifold gauge reads 100 psig on the high side. What is the equivalent pressure in psia at sea level?
options: 114.7 psia
100 psia
85.3 psia
128.4 psia
answer_text: 114.7 psia
explanation: The conversion from gauge pressure to absolute pressure is: psia = psig + 14.696 (≈14.7 at sea level). Therefore, 100 psig + 14.7 = 114.7 psia. Manifold gauges read gauge pressure (psig), which measures pressure relative to atmospheric pressure. Absolute pressure (psia) measures pressure relative to a perfect vacuum and is used in thermodynamic calculations and P-T chart applications.

question: A technician's manifold gauge reads 0 psig. What is the absolute pressure in psia at sea level?
options: 14.7 psia
0 psia
1 psia
29.92 psia
answer_text: 14.7 psia
explanation: At 0 psig, the gauge reads atmospheric pressure — no pressure difference from the atmosphere. Converting to absolute pressure: psia = psig + 14.696 = 0 + 14.7 = 14.7 psia. This represents standard atmospheric pressure at sea level. A reading of 0 psia would indicate a perfect vacuum, which is physically impossible in practice. This conversion is critical when using P-T charts for thermodynamic calculations.

question: A technician measures a low-side pressure of 68.5 psig on an R-22 system. Using a P-T chart, the saturation temperature at that pressure is approximately 40°F. The suction line temperature measured is 50°F. What does this indicate?
options: 10°F of superheat at the evaporator outlet
10°F of subcooling at the condenser
The system is operating at saturation with no superheat
The refrigerant charge is overcharged
answer_text: 10°F of superheat at the evaporator outlet
explanation: Superheat is the temperature rise of refrigerant vapor above its saturation temperature at a given pressure. Here, the suction line measures 50°F while the P-T chart shows the saturation temperature at 68.5 psig is 40°F. Superheat = Actual temperature − Saturation temperature = 50°F − 40°F = 10°F. The P-T chart is the essential tool for determining saturation temperature to calculate superheat.
source_ref: ASHRAE Refrigeration Fundamentals; 40 CFR §82 Technician Guide

question: At saturation conditions, if heat is added to a refrigerant, what happens to the temperature while the refrigerant changes phase from liquid to vapor?
options: The temperature remains constant while phase change occurs
The temperature increases proportionally with heat added
The temperature decreases as vapor pressure drops
The temperature first rises then drops sharply
answer_text: The temperature remains constant while phase change occurs
explanation: At saturation conditions, a refrigerant exists as a mixture of liquid and vapor. When heat is added during phase change, all the energy goes into converting liquid to vapor (latent heat of vaporization) rather than raising the temperature. The temperature remains constant at the saturation temperature until the phase change is complete. This is why P-T charts are useful — at saturation, pressure and temperature have a fixed relationship for each refrigerant.

question: Why must a technician use the correct refrigerant's P-T chart when diagnosing system pressures?
options: Each refrigerant has a unique pressure-temperature relationship at saturation
All HFC refrigerants share the same P-T relationship
P-T charts are only needed for systems with mixed refrigerants
P-T charts apply only to the high-pressure side of the system
answer_text: Each refrigerant has a unique pressure-temperature relationship at saturation
explanation: Each refrigerant has its own unique thermodynamic properties, meaning the saturation pressure at a given temperature varies significantly between refrigerants. For example, R-22 and R-410A have very different saturation pressures at 40°F. Using the wrong P-T chart would lead to incorrect diagnosis of superheat, subcooling, or charge level. Technicians must always identify the refrigerant before using a P-T chart.
source_ref: ASHRAE Refrigeration Fundamentals; 40 CFR §82 Technician Guide

question: A manifold gauge reads 50 psig at sea level. What is the correct formula to convert this to absolute pressure?
options: psia = psig + 14.696
psia = psig − 14.696
psia = psig × 14.696
psia = psig ÷ 14.696
answer_text: psia = psig + 14.696
explanation: The conversion from gauge pressure to absolute pressure is: psia = psig + 14.696 (rounded to 14.7 at sea level). Gauge pressure (psig) measures pressure relative to atmospheric pressure and reads 0 at atmospheric conditions. Absolute pressure (psia) measures relative to a perfect vacuum. Adding the standard atmospheric pressure (14.696 psi) converts psig to psia. For 50 psig: 50 + 14.696 = 64.696 psia.

question: A technician is working at high altitude where atmospheric pressure is 12.5 psia instead of the standard 14.7 psia. How does this affect the psig-to-psia conversion?
options: psia = psig + 12.5, because local atmospheric pressure replaces the standard 14.7 value
psia = psig + 14.7, because atmospheric pressure does not affect gauge readings
psia = psig − 12.5, because reduced atmosphere lowers absolute pressure
psia = psig × 12.5, because the ratio changes at altitude
answer_text: psia = psig + 12.5, because local atmospheric pressure replaces the standard 14.7 value
explanation: The conversion psia = psig + atmospheric pressure uses local atmospheric pressure, not the standard 14.696 psia value. At high altitudes, atmospheric pressure is lower. If local atmospheric pressure is 12.5 psia, then psia = psig + 12.5. This is important for technicians working at elevation because P-T chart values based on sea-level standard conditions may need adjustment, and vacuum gauges may also read differently.

question: Under 40 CFR §82.157(a), what is the annual leak rate threshold that triggers a repair obligation for commercial refrigeration systems?
options: 20% of the full charge per year
10% of the full charge per year
15% of the full charge per year
30% of the full charge per year
answer_text: 20% of the full charge per year
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(a), commercial refrigeration appliances must be repaired when the annual leak rate exceeds 20% of the full refrigerant charge. This threshold is higher than the 10% threshold for comfort cooling systems because commercial refrigeration equipment (such as supermarket cases and walk-in coolers) typically has more complex piping systems that are harder to make completely leak-free.

question: A walk-in cooler has a full refrigerant charge of 150 lbs. Over the past 12 months, 25 lbs of refrigerant was added. Does this trigger a repair obligation under EPA commercial refrigeration standards?
options: Yes, because the 16.7% leak rate exceeds the 20% threshold — wait, 16.7% does not exceed 20%, so no repair is required
Yes, because the 25 lb loss exceeds the 20 lb absolute limit for commercial refrigeration
No, because the annual leak rate of 16.7% does not exceed the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold
Yes, because any walk-in cooler leak must be repaired regardless of leak rate
answer_text: No, because the annual leak rate of 16.7% does not exceed the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold
explanation: Annual leak rate = (25 ÷ 150) × 100 = 16.7%. The threshold for commercial refrigeration under 40 CFR §82.157(a) is 20%. Since 16.7% is below 20%, no repair obligation is triggered at this time. However, the leak should be monitored and documented. Note that comfort cooling systems have a stricter 10% threshold — this same 16.7% rate would trigger a repair obligation if this were an A/C system.

question: Why does EPA set a higher leak rate threshold (20%) for commercial refrigeration compared to comfort cooling (10%)?
options: Commercial refrigeration systems have more complex piping that is inherently harder to seal perfectly
Commercial refrigerants are less harmful to the environment than comfort cooling refrigerants
Commercial refrigeration systems are inspected more frequently, making higher rates acceptable
Commercial refrigeration systems contain smaller charges, making percentage leaks less significant
answer_text: Commercial refrigeration systems have more complex piping that is inherently harder to seal perfectly
explanation: The EPA sets a higher 20% annual leak rate threshold for commercial refrigeration under 40 CFR §82.157(a) because these systems — such as supermarket rack systems, reach-in cases, and walk-in coolers — involve extensive piping networks with many joints, fittings, and service connections. This complexity makes achieving zero leakage technically more challenging than in simpler comfort cooling systems, which have fewer connections and shorter refrigerant lines.

question: A supermarket rack refrigeration system contains 200 lbs of R-404A. The service log shows 45 lbs was added over the past year. What is the annual leak rate, and is repair required?
options: 22.5%; yes, it exceeds the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold
22.5%; no, only systems exceeding 30% must be repaired
22.5%; only if the system also fails a leak inspection
45%; yes, but only if the system contains HFC refrigerant
answer_text: 22.5%; yes, it exceeds the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold
explanation: Annual leak rate = (45 ÷ 200) × 100 = 22.5%. This exceeds the 20% threshold for commercial refrigeration appliances under 40 CFR §82.157(a), so a repair obligation is triggered. The owner must repair the leak within 30 days of discovery per 40 CFR §82.157(b). Supermarket rack systems are clearly classified as commercial refrigeration equipment.

question: Which of the following systems would be subject to the 20% annual leak rate threshold under 40 CFR §82.157(a)?
options: A supermarket reach-in refrigerated display case
A rooftop package air conditioning unit
A residential split-system air conditioner
An industrial ammonia chiller
answer_text: A supermarket reach-in refrigerated display case
explanation: Supermarket reach-in refrigerated display cases are classified as commercial refrigeration equipment and are subject to the 20% annual leak rate threshold under 40 CFR §82.157(a). Rooftop package A/C units and residential split-systems are comfort cooling systems subject to the 10% threshold. Industrial ammonia systems are generally regulated differently and ammonia (R-717) is not covered under Section 608 in the same way as HFCs and HCFCs.

question: A technician is comparing two systems: System A is a comfort cooling unit with a 15% annual leak rate, and System B is a commercial refrigeration unit with a 15% annual leak rate. Which system has a triggered repair obligation under 40 CFR §82.157(a)?
options: System A only, because comfort cooling has a 10% threshold
System B only, because commercial refrigeration has a lower threshold
Both systems, because 15% exceeds both thresholds
Neither system, because 15% is below the universal 20% threshold
answer_text: System A only, because comfort cooling has a 10% threshold
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.157(a), the thresholds differ by system type: comfort cooling systems must repair when leak rate exceeds 10%, while commercial refrigeration systems must repair when leak rate exceeds 20%. At a 15% leak rate, System A (comfort cooling) has exceeded its 10% threshold and repair is required. System B (commercial refrigeration) has not yet exceeded its 20% threshold, so no immediate repair obligation exists for System B.
